Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine this semester. I work part-time and have other responsibilities that always seemed to take priority. When I did study, it was usually late at night after everything else was done, and I'd mostly just read through the materials quickly. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but it never really happened.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything course-related at all, and then I'd have these bursts where I'd try to catch up on everything at once. Deadlines would sometimes jolt me into action, but even then I often missed them. Life stuff just kept getting in the way, and I couldn't establish any consistency.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd realize a deadline was approaching or that I'd missed something important, and that fear would push me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something and that would remind me the course existed. It wasn't a proactive thing for me—more reactive and driven by that scared feeling in my gut.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic or normal. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but then something would come up and I'd fall off. The LMS always felt like this thing I had to force myself to deal with rather than something integrated into my day.
Q5: The reading materials were most useful to me because that's how I learn best—I can print things out or save them and go through them at my own pace. Some of the discussion posts from other students were helpful too because they'd explain concepts in simpler terms. The videos were okay, but I watched a lot of them at double speed just to get through them.
Q6: I ignored most of the optional stuff completely. The discussion forums I barely participated in because I felt like I was already so behind that I had nothing valuable to contribute. Some of the supplementary readings just piled up—I kept telling myself I'd get to them but never did. When you're already drowning, extra materials just feel like more weight.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on the exam or in the assignments. I didn't have the luxury of exploring topics just for interest—I needed to be strategic because my time was so limited. If I couldn't see the immediate payoff, it was hard to justify spending energy on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones at least had that external pressure pushing me, even if I still didn't complete most of them. With optional stuff, there was no consequence for skipping it, so my brain just categorized it as 'not essential' and moved on. The guilt was less with optional things.
Q9: Yes, a lot actually. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone so I could look at them during my commute or breaks at work. I have notebooks where I wrote things out because writing helps me process information better than typing. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just me and my notes.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials on the bus, and obviously none of that shows up anywhere. I'd also sometimes talk through concepts with a friend who took a similar course, which helped me understand things better. The LMS only sees what happens online, but my learning often happened offline.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a reading or save a video, and then I'd come back to it multiple times over the following days without ever logging back in. My LMS activity probably looked like I glanced at something once and moved on, but in reality I might have spent hours with that same material offline. It's just more practical for me that way.
Q12: Around midterm time, everything fell apart. I was dealing with some personal stuff, my work hours increased, and I just felt completely overwhelmed. The fear of how far behind I'd gotten made it even harder to log in because I didn't want to face it. It became this vicious cycle where avoiding the course made me more anxious, which made me avoid it more.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work, the last thing I wanted to do was stare at a screen and try to absorb complex information. There were weeks where I was so mentally drained that even logging in felt like too much. My limited resources meant I couldn't just quit my job to focus on school, so the LMS always came last in my priorities.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in, open a video, and just zone out completely because my mind was elsewhere—worrying about bills, thinking about tomorrow's shift. I'd scroll through readings without actually processing any of it. My eyes were on the screen but my brain wasn't engaged at all. It was just going through the motions.
Q15: All the time. I'd see an assignment and feel this wave of anxiety about whether I could even do it, so I'd close the tab and tell myself I'd handle it later. The CBT sessions I had helped me recognize this pattern—it's avoidance driven by fear of failure. Knowing that didn't always stop me from doing it though.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that sometimes got me moving, but even then I missed most of them. I'd see a deadline approaching and feel paralyzed rather than motivated. When I did engage close to deadlines, it was frantic and stressful rather than productive. The structure of deadlines didn't work well with my chaotic schedule.
Q17: When I actually submitted something and got decent feedback, it did motivate me briefly. My assignment scores were pretty good when I actually completed things, which was encouraging. But the positive feelings never lasted long enough to build momentum. The gap between feedback and my next engagement was usually too long.
Q18: The course structure assumed a level of consistent availability that I just didn't have. Having 19 courses worth of content to manage was overwhelming from the start. The pacing didn't account for people like me who can't dedicate regular hours each week. I felt like the structure was designed for a different kind of student than I am.
Q19: The flexibility of online materials was good in theory—I could access things whenever. But the sheer volume made it hard to know where to focus. Having clearer priorities or a 'minimum viable path' through the content would have helped. The discussion requirements also felt awkward when I was so far behind everyone else.
Q20: I think sometimes you're searching for something, trying to understand, clicking around looking for the thing that will make it click. You might watch videos and read materials but still feel lost about what the assignment actually wants. High activity doesn't mean high understanding—sometimes it means high confusion.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. When I actually sat down with materials offline and really focused, I could learn efficiently. My exam score wasn't great, but my assignment scores were decent when I submitted. Some people just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn—they work differently. The LMS doesn't capture that.
Q22: A course log can show when someone logged in and what they clicked on, but it can't show understanding or effort. It misses all the offline work, the mental processing, the real-life barriers. It also can't distinguish between someone mindlessly scrolling and someone deeply engaged. It's just a shadow of what's actually happening.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and thinking about the material, even when you're not actively studying. It's when concepts stick with you and you find yourself connecting them to other things. For me, engagement was sporadic—there were moments of genuine interest buried under all the stress and avoidance. True engagement requires mental space I rarely had.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my fear—how scared I was of failing, of wasting money I don't have, of confirming that maybe I'm not cut out for this. It wouldn't show the offline studying or the genuine interest I had in some topics. It definitely wouldn't show how hard I was trying to manage everything else in my life while attempting this course. The data just shows absence, not the reasons behind it.
